TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have remained' denotes a stative situation (continuous presence/staying) rather than a bounded transition or completed event; it presents an ongoing state over a duration ('three days') without construing an endpoint as the focus.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have remained' is asserted as a realized fact within the actual world of the narrative (the disciples have actually stayed with Jesus for three days), not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have remained' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle) with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have remained' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + present participle 'remaining' understood as perfect aspect), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent performing the action of remaining, not a patient undergoing an action.

FullText: When they came down from the mountain , Jesus commanded them not to tell anyone what they saw , until the Son of man has risen from the dead .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive with no overt direct object; it takes only an external argument (the Son of man) and does not license an internal argument, failing all three set_to_1_if conditions.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event (resurrection) where the sole argument 'Son of man' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' is unaccusative (denoting a change of state/coming-into-being), where the subject 'Son of man' functions as an internal argument rather than an external agent/initiator, thus failing to meet ASUnrg criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has risen from the dead' denotes a punctual boundary transition (resurrection as a singular culminating event without an inherent durative process phase), analogous to 'arrive', making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (crossing the threshold from death to life), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, and represents a definable boundary event rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has risen from the dead' denotes a transition to a defined result state (being alive after death) with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' entails a resulting change of state (transition from dead to alive), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the intuition and set_to_1_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has risen from the dead' denotes a bounded, dynamic event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of rising), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram occurs in a temporal clause ('until the Son of man has risen from the dead') expressing a future condition that is expected to be fulfilled, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen from the dead' contains no negation markers ('not', 'n't', negative quantifiers, or 'never'), and the negation in the sentence ('not to tell') applies to a different verb, not the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'until', a temporal conjunction that explicitly anchors the event 'has risen from the dead' to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'has risen from the dead' introduces a resulting state (being alive/resurrected) that is relevant for the subsequent discourse condition ('until the Son of man has risen'), making it non-experiential narrative progression rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) where the resulting state (being risen/alive) is currently in effect at the reference time, matching the motion verb criterion where the mover has moved to a new state and remains there.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen from the dead' denotes a punctual, perfective event (a transition into a new state) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and no duration adverbials are present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen from the dead' is a transition event (rise = change of state) construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen from the dead' appears in a temporal clause ('until the Son of man has risen from the dead') that projects a future event as a condition for when the prohibition ends, presenting the situation as not yet realized at the time of speaking.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'risen') with no tense shift, modal, future marking, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'risen' is an intransitive past participle, and the subject 'Son of man' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: » I tell you that Elijah has come . They did not know him and treated him badly as they pleased . They will do the same with the Son of man . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive with no overt direct object or internal argument, failing all three set_to_1_if conditions.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Elijah) undergoes displacement and no external argument is licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for motion verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' is unaccusative (the subject 'Elijah' is an internal argument undergoing a change of state/location), not an unergative verb with an external agent argument, as shown in the negative examples where 'fallen' and 'appeared' are similarly classified as 0.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a destination), not a durative process leading toward a goal, analogous to the negative example 'The guests have arrived.'
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location or into presence), satisfying the criterion of a near-instantaneous change-of-state event that crosses a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a destination) and a defined result state (being present), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a resulting change of state (Elijah's arrival and presence) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a bounded dynamic event with an inherent endpoint (arrival), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has come' is a factual statement about Elijah's arrival, not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional; it describes an event understood to have occurred in the narrative world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; it appears in a positive assertion ('Elijah has come'), and negation in the following clause ('did not know') applies to a different verb, not the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Elijah has come' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial to anchor the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'has come' introduces a result state (Elijah's arrival/presence) that is relevant for the subsequent discourse about how he was treated, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes a change of location where the mover (Elijah) has arrived and the resulting state (his presence) persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent clause 'They did not know him and treated him badly.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Elijah has come' denotes a punctual, completed arrival event without duration adverbials, habitual iteration, or continuous state extension to the reference time, matching the negative example pattern of bounded/punctual results.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' is asserted as a realized fact within the speaker's statement ('I tell you that Elijah has come'), not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has come' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'come') with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'come'), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » What do you think ? A man has a hundred sheep , and one of them has gone wandering away . Will he leave the ninety-nine and go in search of the wandering one ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' is intransitive with no overt direct object; 'wandering' is a participial modifier describing the manner of going, not an internal argument, so the construction lacks the required two-participant transitive structure.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (one sheep) undergoes displacement without licensing an external argument, matching the unaccusative pattern of verbs like 'arrive' and 'fall'.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes manner of motion with the subject 'one of them' (the sheep) functioning as the external argument and no internal argument licensed, matching the third set_to_1_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has gone wandering away' describes a durative process of directed motion away from the flock toward an unintended but defined spatial endpoint (being lost/away), fitting the accomplishment profile of directed motion toward a goal that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'has gone wandering' denotes an ongoing durative process of wandering rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it lacks the instantaneous boundary crossing characteristic of achievements and permits internal process duration.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' denotes a completed change of state (the sheep has departed and is now in a wandering state), with an inherent endpoint marking the transition from being with the flock to being separated, which disqualifies it as an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has gone wandering' entails a resulting change of state (the sheep is now in a wandering condition away from the flock) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence, thus it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has gone wandering' denotes a dynamic, bounded event of movement with internal progression (the sheep departed and wandered away), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' describes a factual situation within a hypothetical scenario (a parable), not a counterfactual condition; it is presented as an actual event that occurred, not as contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has gone wandering' contains no negation marker (not/n't), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; it presents the event as realized, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'one of them has gone wandering away' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering away' introduces a resulting state (the sheep is currently lost/separated) that is relevant for the subsequent narrative question about whether the man will search for it, making this a narrative progression rather than an experiential entry in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'gone wandering' describes a change of location where the sheep has moved away from the flock and remains in that displaced state at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition for motion verbs.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has gone wandering away' describes a punctual transition into a new state (the sheep becoming lost/wandering) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has gone wandering' presents an ongoing or habitual state rather than a single bounded occurrence; the progressive participle 'wandering' construes the situation as continuous activity, not a completed transition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact within the narrative scenario (the sheep has actually wandered away), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'gone'), which does not instantiate any of the tense-shifted conditions (no past auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive 'been' + participle).
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has gone wandering' uses 'gone' (past participle of 'go') rather than the required 'been' + past participle sequence for passive voice, and 'wandering' functions as a complement describing the subject's action, not passive morphology marking the subject as semantic patient.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have been filled with joy' is a passive construction where the lexical verb 'fill' licenses both an external argument (implicit agent) and an internal argument (the subject 'he' as patient), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'would have been filled with joy' is a passive construction where 'filled' is the past participle of a transitive verb that licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the filling), making it not unaccusative despite the copular 'be'.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a passive construction where the subject 'he' functions as an internal argument (patient/theme) receiving the state of being filled, not as an external argument/agent initiating an action, thus failing the ASUnrg criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: Being filled with joy describes a punctual transition into a resultant emotional state rather than a durative process unfolding toward a defined endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a durative state of being filled with joy rather than a punctual transition into that state; it lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing quality required for AktAch classification.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' denotes a result state (being filled with joy) rather than a dynamic, durative activity; it lacks the ongoing, homogeneous character required for AktAct and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' expresses a resulting state of being filled with joy, which entails a change of state and is not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' denotes a mental/emotional state (joy) that would hold over time without dynamic change or internal progression, satisfying the criterion for stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'if he had stayed', expressing an unfulfilled hypothetical outcome contrary to the actual situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have (been filled with joy)' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it expresses a positive conditional state.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'if he had stayed', which is a conditional temporal clause that anchors the counterfactual event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical result state (being filled with joy) rather than presenting an event as part of the subject's life history; it does not meet any of the set_to_1_if conditions.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have been filled') describing a hypothetical state that did not occur, not an actual change of state with a persisting result at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical state in an unrealized past scenario, not a situation extending continuously from a past point to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a conditional perfect passive construction that presents a bounded transition event (the state of being filled with joy as a completed whole) as a hypothetical but bounded occurrence, matching the perfective construal of a transition with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a modal auxiliary construction ('would have') that presents a counterfactual/hypothetical situation as non-realized, appearing in a conditional structure ('if he had stayed...he would have...') that explicitly construes the situation as contrary-to-fact rather than asserted as actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have' includes a modal auxiliary (would) preceding 'have', which instantiates the TLTS feature as a modal perfect construction with tense-shifted finite operator.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is a conditional modal construction without the 'been' element necessary for passive voice marking.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'stayed' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt direct object; 'with the ninety-nine' is a prepositional phrase indicating location/accompaniment, not a grammatical object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'stayed' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as an agent who deliberately remains in a location), and the sole overt argument functions as an external argument rather than an internal theme/patient argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'stayed' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'he' functioning as an external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in an activity without licensing an internal argument, matching the set_to_1_if condition for volitional agents without internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Had stayed' denotes a durative but unbounded state (staying with the ninety-nine) without a defined terminal endpoint or culminating result state, making it a stative/activity rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' denotes a durative state or condition (remaining in a location/situation) with inherent internal duration, not a punctual transition into a new state, and does not satisfy any of the set_to_1_if conditions.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of remaining in a location over time without an inherent endpoint, satisfying the criterion of a situation that can be modified by durative adverbials (e.g., 'had stayed for hours') and is homogeneous in nature.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had stayed' denotes a durative state or activity (remaining in a location over time) that entails a resulting condition, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state, so it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' denotes a bounded, completed action of remaining in a location over a specific past period, which involves dynamic temporal progression and a defined endpoint, failing to meet the stative criteria of holding without inherent progression or change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had stayed' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if he had stayed') expressing an unfulfilled past condition contrary to fact, paired with the apodosis 'he would have' indicating what would have resulted under different circumstances.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had stayed' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause without negation markers ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' constructions.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'when' (temporal conjunction) at the beginning of the sentence, which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had stayed' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('if he had stayed') that establishes a hypothetical result state (remaining with the ninety-nine) for comparison, not an experiential entry in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' is a stative verb describing a continuous state of remaining in a location within a counterfactual conditional clause; it does not encode a change of state or a resulting state currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'if he had stayed with the ninety-nine' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical alternative state, not a situation that held continuously from a past point to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'if he had stayed with the ninety-nine' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized, ongoing state rather than a bounded completed event; 'stayed' denotes a durative situation without an asserted endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had stayed' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('he would have if he had stayed'), presenting a hypothetical alternative to the realized situation, which instantiates the irrealis feature.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form that instantiates tense-locus-tense-shift (TLTS) rather than a simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had stayed' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle of intransitive verb 'stay'), not passive voice; passive voice requires the morphological sequence 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » If your brother does wrong to you , go to him in private . Clearly explain his error between you and him . If he listens to you , you have gained your brother back .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have gained back' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('your brother'), instantiating a two-participant transitive event directed from agent to patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gain back' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') that actively obtains or recovers the brother, making it transitive with an agentive external argument rather than an unaccusative verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' licenses an internal argument ('your brother'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have gained your brother back' describes a directed process of reconciliation leading to a defined result state (the brother being restored to the relationship), fitting the accomplishment profile of a durative process culminating in a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (regaining one's brother through reconciliation), which is a definable threshold event that cannot be interrupted mid-occurrence and naturally occurs at a specific temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have gained back' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint (recovering/regaining one's brother) and a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have gained back' entails a resulting change of state (regaining a brother's relationship/presence), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; semelfactives must be punctual events without consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have gained back' denotes a dynamic, bounded achievement event (regaining/recovering a relationship) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'you have gained your brother back' is the apodosis of an open conditional ('If he listens to you'), expressing a possible future outcome rather than a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have gained back' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents a positive event as occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'you have gained your brother back' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'you have gained your brother back' introduces a resulting state (reconciliation/restoration of the brother relationship) that is relevant for the conditional discourse, not a life-history experiential event.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' describes a change of state (regaining/recovering a relationship with the brother) where the resulting state (having the brother back) is currently in effect at the reference time, as indicated by the conditional context 'If he listens to you, you have gained your brother back.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'you have gained your brother back' describes a punctual result or transition into a new state (regaining a brother) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have gained back' construes a transition event (regaining/recovering a brother) as a completed bounded whole, functioning as a discrete narrative outcome in the conditional sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have gained back' appears in the consequent of a conditional ('If he listens to you, you have gained your brother back') and is asserted as a realized outcome within that conditional scenario, not as a hypothetical or projected situation; it expresses a factual result rather than irrealis modality.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have gained back' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle 'gained'), with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have gained back' is active voice (agent subject 'you' performs the action of gaining), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'have + been + past participle' morphological structure for VcPass.

FullText: » When the other servants saw what happened , they were very sad . They told their lord what had been done .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been done'), and the lexical verb 'do' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, implied by the passive construction), satisfying the second 'set_to_1_if' condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction that licenses an implicit external argument (the agent performing the action), which disqualifies it from ASUncc status despite having a single overt argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction where the grammatical subject is the internal argument (patient/theme) of the action, not the external argument (agent), thus failing the ASUnrg criteria which requires the sole argument to function as an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'what had been done' is a vague reference to an unspecified action reported to the lord, lacking any indication of a durative process directed toward a defined goal or endpoint that would characterize an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' describes a completed durative process or state rather than a punctual transition; it reports what action was performed without construing the event as a boundary-crossing moment, and cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in this context.
AktAct_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' denotes a completed action with a defined result state, not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive construction describing a completed action with a resulting change of state (something was done to someone/something), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' denotes a completed, bounded dynamic event (the action of doing something), not a stative situation that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a factual past perfect in an indirect report clause ('They told their lord what had been done'), describing an event that actually occurred, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' contains no negation marker ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' is embedded within a larger sentence that contains the clausal past time anchor 'When the other servants saw what happened', which uses the temporal conjunction 'when' to anchor the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' introduces a result state (an action completed and its consequences) that is relevant for the narrative progression—the servants report this to their lord because the resulting situation matters for the plot, not to mark an experiential entry in a life history.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' is a stative copula construction (past participle with 'been') that describes a state rather than encoding a transition, and the context shows it merely reports a past interaction without indicating a persisting resultative state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' describes a completed punctual action (a discrete event that occurred and was finished) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' presents a passive bounded transition event (the completion of an action) as a single whole, matching the criterion for transition events construed as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a past perfect passive form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (what the servants reported to their lord), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, and the gram includes 'been' followed by a past participle 'done' (passive progressive structure), satisfying two set_to_1_if conditions: past auxiliary marking and the 'been' + participle pattern.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates the passive voice construction with the auxiliary sequence 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'done' of a transitive verb, meeting the first set_to_1_if condition.

FullText: » So they are no longer two , but one flesh . What God has joined together , let no man pull apart separate ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' has an overt grammatical subject ('God') and an overt direct object ('together' modifies the action on the implied object referent 'them/two'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with both subject and object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'join together' licenses an external argument (God as agent performing the action of joining), making it transitive rather than unaccusative; the sole overt argument functions as an internal argument but an external argument is still licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' licenses an internal argument ('together' modifies the object relationship, and the construction implies a patient/theme argument receiving the action of joining), failing the requirement of no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has joined together' denotes a punctual uniting act (a boundary transition into a unified state) rather than a durative process unfolding toward a goal, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the union of two entities into one), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a punctual, perfective action with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (the joining is complete), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has joined together' entails a resulting change of state (two becoming one flesh), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI definition requiring 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a dynamic event of uniting two entities into one, which involves internal change and progression from a separated state to a unified state, failing all four set_to_1_if conditions for stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has joined together' occurs in a relative clause describing God's actual action (not a counterfactual condition), and does not instantiate any of the set_to_1_if conditions for counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has joined together' is not negated; negation appears only on the subsequent clause 'let no man pull apart' which is a separate predicate.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'What God has joined together' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'has joined together' introduces a resulting state (the couple being united as 'one flesh') that is relevant for the subsequent discourse ('let no man pull apart'), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' describes a change of state (union of two into one flesh) with the resulting state currently in effect, as evidenced by the immediate context 'they are no longer two, but one flesh' and the directive 'let no man pull apart' which presupposes the joined state persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has joined together' denotes a punctual, completed action resulting in a state change (joining two into one flesh), not a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has joined together' is a transition event (joining is a change-of-state predicate) construed as a completed whole, asserting a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has joined together' is a present perfect asserting a realized action by God in the actual world, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; it appears in a relative clause describing an established fact, not in a modal, conditional, or optative construction.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has joined together' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'joined'), which does not instantiate any of the tense-shifted conditions (no past auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive 'been').
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has joined together' is active voice (subject 'God' is the agent performing the action), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'have + been + past participle' morphological structure for VcPass.

FullText: He said : » This does not apply to everyone , only to those God has given it to .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has given' is ditransitive, licensing both an indirect object ('it') and an implied direct recipient ('to those'), satisfying the two-participant transitive requirement with overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an external argument (God as agent) and an internal argument (it as theme), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria of a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('it'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has given' denotes a punctual transfer/bestowal without an inherent durative process phase leading to completion, analogous to 'He has paid the server' in the negative examples.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/ability) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a punctual, telic event with a defined result state (the transfer of something from God to recipients), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.

AktState_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed dynamic action (transfer of possession) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.

DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'has given' introduces a resulting state (God's possession/bestowal of an ability or quality to certain people) that is relevant for the subsequent discourse about who the rule applies to, rather than presenting the event as part of a life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given' describes a change of possession (God has given something to certain people), and the context entails that this possession is currently held at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'God has given it to' describes a discrete past action with a resultant state, not a continuous situation extending to the reference time; there is no duration adverbial, habitual series, or 'since' construction indicating universal temporal extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has given' is a transition event (giving is a completed transfer) construed as a bounded whole, asserting a completed action rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given' is embedded in a relative clause describing a realized state of affairs (those to whom God has given it), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; it asserts a completed action as fact within the speaker's reported statement.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has given' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'given') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'give'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'God' is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient.

FullText: » For there are men who , from birth , were without sex . There are some who were made that way by men . There are others who have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven . He who is able to accept this let him accept it . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('others' / 'who') and an overt direct object ('themselves'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with both subject and object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have made' licenses an external argument ('themselves' as the agent performing the action of making), so it does not meet the criterion of a verb that fails to license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have made' takes a reflexive pronoun 'themselves' as its only argument (functioning as both subject and internal argument in reflexive construction), but the core predicate denotes a volitional agent performing an action without an external object, matching the pattern of agentive unergative verbs where the subject is the external argument initiating the activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have made themselves without sex' denotes a deliberate, directed process toward a defined result state (being without sex for the kingdom of heaven), which involves a durative process culminating in a bounded outcome, satisfying the accomplishment criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have made themselves' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (becoming without sex), represents a definable threshold crossing, and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have made themselves' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (becoming without sex for the kingdom of heaven), not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have made themselves' entails a resulting change of state (becoming without sex) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence, thus it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have made themselves' denotes a dynamic, volitional action with internal development and an inherent endpoint (the state of being without sex), not a static condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' describes a factual action (a real choice people have made) rather than a counterfactual situation, and does not occur in a protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have made' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the clause describes a realized action rather than an absent one.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have made themselves' denotes a bounded activity (self-castration or renunciation) where the focus is on having undergone this experience as part of the subject's life history, rather than on a persisting physical result state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have made themselves' describes a change of state (becoming without sex) where the resulting state persists at the reference time, as indicated by the context 'for the kingdom of heaven' which implies the state is currently maintained.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' describes a discrete, completed action (self-castration) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials and denoting a punctual transition into a new state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have made themselves' construes a reflexive transition event (self-castration or renunciation) as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have made themselves' is a present perfect asserting a realized action in the actual world (those who have already made themselves that way), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have made' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle 'made') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have made themselves' is active voice (agent 'they' as subject performing the action of making), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: The man said to him : » I have done all these things ; what more is there ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have done' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('all these things'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with two participants.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I'), making this an agentive transitive construction rather than an unaccusative one-participant event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('all these things' as direct object), so it does not meet the requirement of having no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have done all these things' implies a durative process of completing a defined set of tasks or actions directed toward a cumulative goal, fitting the accomplishment profile of a bounded event with a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have done' denotes a completed action with inherent process phases (the various things accomplished), not a punctual transition into a new state; it describes an extended accomplishment rather than a boundary-crossing moment.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have done' with a bounded internal argument 'all these things' denotes a completed action with a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' describes a completed action with a resulting state (things accomplished), not a single punctual occurrence without change of state, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' denotes a completed dynamic action with internal progression and an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'have done' is a factual statement in direct speech reporting a completed action in the actual world, not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have done' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the clause presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have done all these things' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'have done all these things' introduces a resulting state of completion and accomplishment relevant to the man's response ('what more is there?'), rather than presenting the events as part of life history without asserting a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'do' describes completed actions without encoding a persisting resultant state; the clause reports past accomplishments without indicating that a changed state currently holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have done all these things' describes completed discrete actions rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or non-terminating predicates required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have done all these things' presents a completed set of actions as a bounded whole with a construed endpoint, construing the accomplishment of multiple actions as a single perfective event rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done' is asserted as a realized fact within direct speech ('I have done all these things'), not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have done' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle 'done') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, matching the canonical form that does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have done' is active voice (present perfect of transitive verb 'do'), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and the grammatical subject 'I' is the agent, not the semantic patient.

FullText: Then Peter said : » See , we have given up everything to follow you . What then will we have ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have given up' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('we') and an overt direct object ('everything'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with two participants.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give up' licenses an external argument (we/Peter) as an agent performing a volitional action, and takes an internal argument (everything) as the theme; this is a transitive agentive construction, not an unaccusative one-participant event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'give up' licenses an internal argument ('everything'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Give up everything' denotes a punctual relinquishment/renunciation event — a boundary transition into a state of having abandoned possessions — rather than a durative process unfolding toward a defined endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'give up' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (abandonment of possessions/commitments), can be modified by time-point adverbials ('we gave up everything at that moment'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot meaningfully stop giving up'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have given up' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (the state of having relinquished everything), not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have given up' entails a resulting change of state (transition from possessing to not possessing), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI definition requiring 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have given up' denotes a completed dynamic action with an inherent endpoint (the act of abandoning/relinquishing), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have given up' is a factual statement in a direct speech context describing what Peter and his companions actually did (followed Jesus and gave up possessions), not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have given up' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the clause presents the event as realized, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'we have given up everything to follow you' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverb anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; the initial 'Then' is a logical connector ('therefore'), not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'have given up' introduces a resulting state (the disciples are now in a condition of having abandoned their possessions and livelihoods to follow Jesus), which is relevant for the subsequent discourse about what they will receive in return, rather than focusing on the occurrence of the event as a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'give up' describes a change of state (abandonment of possessions/attachments), and the context 'we have given up everything to follow you' indicates that this resulting state of having relinquished everything persists at the reference time as the basis for their current commitment to following Jesus.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'we have given up everything' describes a completed action with a resultant state rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time; it lacks duration adverbials and denotes a punctual transition, not a non-terminating activity.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have given up' is a transition event (giving up = change of state from possessing to relinquishing) construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents this as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have given up' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (Peter states what they have actually done), not as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have given up' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle) with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have given up' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'give up'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'we' is the semantic agent performing the action of giving up, not the patient being affected.

FullText: Jesus replied : » Truly I say to you , when all things are made new , and the Son of man is seated in his glory , you who have come after me will be seated on twelve seats , judging the twelve tribes of Israel .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have come' is intransitive with no overt direct object; 'after me' is a prepositional phrase indicating direction, not a grammatical object, so the two-participant transitive pattern required for ASTrns is not instantiated.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'you' undergoes displacement (coming after Jesus) with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for motion verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' is unaccusative (the subject 'you' is an internal argument undergoing a change of state/location), not an unergative verb with an external agent argument, as evidenced by the negative example 'He has fallen' which is similarly unaccusative.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have come after me' denotes directed motion/following toward a goal (discipleship), construed as a durative process of following Jesus leading to a defined relational endpoint, fitting the accomplishment profile of directed action toward an abstract goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/following after Jesus), which is a definable threshold event that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have come after me' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of coming), not a dynamic durative activity; it describes a bounded change of state rather than an ongoing, homogeneous situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have come after me' describes a resultant state (followers who have arrived and remain in that condition) rather than a single punctual occurrence without change of state, thus it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have come' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (arrival/movement) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'have come after me' describes a factual or future state of followers who will exist, not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact; it occurs in a relative clause describing actual disciples, not in a protasis, apodosis, or unfulfilled wish construction.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have come' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents a positive event.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'when' (temporal conjunction) in 'when all things are made new', which explicitly anchors the event of 'have come' to a specific past time interval relative to the future scenario described.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'you who have come after me' describes a resulting state (followers who are positioned after Jesus) relevant to the subsequent clause about being seated on twelve seats, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'have come after me' describes a past action of following Jesus without indicating that a resulting state persists at the reference time; it reports a past relationship rather than a current change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have come after me' describes a discrete, completed action of following Jesus, not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or 'since' markers indicating universal temporal extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have come' is construed as a completed transition event (the act of coming/following after Jesus), presented as a bounded whole rather than ongoing, matching the criterion for transition events asserted as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' is a relative clause modifying 'you' and asserts a realized fact (those who have actually followed Jesus), not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have come' is a simple present perfect form with no past auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have come' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'come'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » Everyone who has given up houses , or brothers , or sisters , or father , or mother , or child , or land , for my name , will receive many times as much , and have everlasting life .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has given up' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Everyone') and overt direct objects ('houses, or brothers, or sisters, or father, or mother, or child, or land'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a two-participant transitive event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give up' licenses an external argument (the agent 'everyone' who performs the action of relinquishing), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given up' is transitive and licenses an internal argument (the objects 'houses, brothers, sisters, father, mother, child, land'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The act of giving up (renouncing/relinquishing) houses, family, or land is construed as a punctual volitional decision or transition rather than a durative process unfolding toward a defined endpoint, making it more achievement-like than accomplishment-like.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has given up' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (relinquishing possession/relationship), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('gave up at that moment'), and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given up' denotes a punctual, telic action with an inherent endpoint and result state (the state of having relinquished possessions), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has given up' entails a resulting change of state (transition from possessing to not possessing), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; additionally, the context allows iterative interpretation (multiple people giving up multiple items), making it a durative activity rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has given up' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (relinquishment of possessions) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has given up' occurs in the protasis of a conditional, but it describes a factual or open possibility (those who have actually given up things), not a counterfactual situation understood not to have occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has given up' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier modifying it directly, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the negative quantifier 'Everyone who' is a universal quantifier in a conditional structure, not a negation of the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'has given up houses, or brothers, or sisters...' introduces a resulting state (the subject no longer possesses these things) that is relevant for the subsequent clause 'will receive many times as much,' making this a narrative progression establishing preconditions rather than an experiential entry in life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given up' describes a change of state (relinquishment of possessions/relationships) where the resulting state of having relinquished persists at the reference time, and the context indicates this change is currently in effect as a condition for receiving the promised reward.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has given up' denotes a punctual, completed action resulting in a change of state (transition from possessing to not possessing), not a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has given up' is a transition event (relinquishment/abandonment construed as a completed whole) presented as a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, satisfying the criteria for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given up' is a present perfect asserting a realized action in the actual world; the irrealis marking ('will receive') applies to the consequent clause, not to this antecedent condition.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has given up' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'given up') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given up' is active voice (subject 'Everyone' is the agent performing the action of giving up), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'have + been + past participle' morphology for VcPass.

FullText: » They said : ‘ No man has given us work . ’ ’ He told them to go work with the rest in the vineyard .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has given' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('No man') and an overt direct object ('us work'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a two-participant transitive event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an external argument (the agent/giver) and an internal argument (the recipient/theme), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria of a sole internal argument with no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('work' as direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has given work' denotes a punctual transfer/act of giving without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined culmination point, analogous to 'he has paid the server' rather than a gradual accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'give work' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (employment/assignment), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('gave work at dawn'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot meaningfully stop giving work'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has given work' denotes a completed transfer of work (a bounded, quantized internal argument), which entails a defined result state rather than a dynamic, durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has given us work' entails a resulting change of state (the workers now have employment/work assigned to them), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; semelfactives must be punctual events without consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has given work' denotes a completed dynamic event (giving is a volitional action with internal progression and a defined endpoint), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has given work' occurs in a reported statement ('They said: No man has given us work') describing a factual situation in the past, not a counterfactual condition, unfulfilled wish, or contrary-to-fact scenario.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has given work' is negated by the negative quantifier 'No man' in subject position, which satisfies the second set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'No man has given us work' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'has not given us work' functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subjects' life history, matching the set_to_1_if condition for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'No man has given us work' reports a past interaction (lack of employment opportunity) without entailing that a directive or binding state currently persists; it merely describes a historical absence of work provision.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'No man has given us work' describes a punctual or bounded event (the absence of a giving action) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or 'since' markers indicating universal temporal extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has given us work' presents a bounded completion event (the act of giving work as a single accomplished whole) rather than an ongoing or habitual situation, matching the perfective construal of a completed transition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given work' is a present perfect assertion of a realized state of affairs in the actual world (no one has given them work), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has given work' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'given') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given work' is active voice (subject 'No man' is the agent performing the action), not passive voice; it lacks the required auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » They said : ‘ The last ones have done only one hour’s work . You have made them equal to us . We have undergone the hard work of the day and the burning heat . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have done' has an overt grammatical subject ('The last ones') and an overt direct object ('one hour's work'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have done' licenses an external argument ('the last ones') that functions as an agent performing work, not as an internal argument undergoing a change of state or motion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have done' takes an overt subject ('The last ones') functioning as an external argument (agent performing work), and while 'work' appears as a noun complement, it functions as a cognate object describing the activity itself rather than a true internal argument, making this consistent with ASUnrg as an unergative construction denoting volitional activity.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have done one hour's work' describes a durative process directed toward a defined, measurable goal (completing one hour's worth of work), fitting the accomplishment profile with an inherent endpoint defined by the quantity of work.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have done (work)' denotes a durative activity with an extended process phase rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it describes the completion of labor over time, not a boundary-crossing moment.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have done only one hour's work' denotes a bounded, completed action with a quantized internal argument ('one hour's work'), indicating an inherent endpoint rather than a dynamic durative activity without defined completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have done only one hour's work' describes a completed durative activity (work performed over an hour) that entails a resulting change of state (work accomplished), not a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done (work)' denotes a completed dynamic action with internal progression and a bounded endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'have done' describes a factual past event (workers completed one hour of work) in reported speech, not a counterfactual condition, unfulfilled wish, or contrary-to-fact scenario.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have done' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'The last ones have done only one hour's work' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have done only one hour's work' introduces a result state (the workers have completed one hour of work as a factual accomplishment affecting the current situation), not a life-history entry, and lacks frequency adverbs or perception/communication focus that would trigger DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have done' describes a completed action (work performed) without indicating a persisting change of state; the clause reports a past activity without entailing that a resulting state currently holds at the reference time.

FullText: They said : » We are able . « » Truly , you will take of my cup , « he said . » But to be seated at my right hand and at my left is not for me to give . It is for those for whom it has been prepared by my Father . «

ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'have you read' is a factual question about past experience in the actual world, not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have read' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it is a positive interrogative form.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'have you read that which was spoken to you by God' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'have you read that which was spoken to you by God?' is a perception verb (read) focusing on whether the experience of reading has occurred at least once in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if condition for perception verbs where the focus is on occurrence rather than resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have read' reports a past interaction (reading a text) without indicating that a persisting resultative state is currently in effect; the clause merely asks whether the addressee has encountered the text, not whether reading it produces a binding or changed state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have you read that which was spoken to you by God' describes a discrete, completed reading event without duration adverbials, habitual iteration, or continuous state extension to the reference time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have you read that which was spoken to you by God' presents reading as an unbounded experiential state (asking whether the addressee has encountered the text at any point) rather than as a single bounded completion, and 'read' is not a transition predicate in this context.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The interrogative 'have you read' seeks confirmation of an actual fact (whether the addressee has read a specific text), not a hypothetical or projected situation, and contains no modal auxiliary or conditional marking.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have read' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle 'read') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have read' is active voice (subject 'you' is the agent performing the reading action), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: The Pharisees assembled together when they heard that he had put the Sadducees to silence .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') and an overt direct object ('the Sadducees'), encoding a two-participant transitive event directed from agent toward patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' licenses an external argument (the agent 'he' who performs the action of silencing) and an internal argument (the Sadducees who undergo the silencing), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' licenses an internal argument (the Sadducees as direct object), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Put to silence' describes a directed process of suppressing or defeating the Sadducees in argument, culminating in a defined result state (their silence), which aligns with a durative accomplishment directed toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (causing someone to become silent), which is a definable threshold event that occurs at a temporal boundary and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' denotes a punctual, telic action with an inherent endpoint (the state of being silenced), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'put to silence' entails a resulting change of state (the Sadducees transitioning from speaking to being silenced), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI definition requiring 'no resulting change of state.'
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had put to silence' denotes a dynamic, bounded event of causing someone to become silent, involving volitional action and internal change, which does not meet any of the stative criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had put to silence' is a factual past event in an indirect report clause ('when they heard that he had put...'), not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had put to silence' contains no negation marker (not/n't), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; it presents the event as realized, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'when' (temporal conjunction) that explicitly anchors the event 'had put to silence' to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'had put the Sadducees to silence' describes a completed action that sets the scene for the subsequent narrative event ('The Pharisees assembled together'), making the resulting state (Sadducees being silenced) relevant for discourse progression rather than presenting the event as part of a life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' describes a change of state (from speaking to silenced), and the clause indicates that the Sadducees remained in that silenced state at the reference time when the Pharisees heard about it, satisfying the resultative condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'had put to silence' denotes a punctual, completed action (silencing the Sadducees) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and no duration adverbials are present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'put to silence' is a transition event (causing a change of state from speaking to silence) construed as a completed whole in past perfect aspect, presenting a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had put to silence' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the Pharisees heard that he had silenced the Sadducees), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
