DtRes_explanation: The conditional construction 'would have watched' expresses a counterfactual hypothetical action that did not occur, not a change of state with a persisting result at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical punctual action (watching at a specific hour), not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'would have watched' presents a hypothetical bounded completion (the act of watching as a single completed event in response to knowing the thief's arrival time), construed as a discrete protective action rather than an ongoing or habitual state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' appears in a conditional construction ('if the master of the house had known...he would have watched') expressing a counterfactual/hypothetical situation, which instantiates irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' includes a modal auxiliary ('would') preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if condition for modal perfect constructions that are tense-shifted from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is active voice (agent as subject, transitive verb in past participle form without 'been'), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'allowed' licenses two arguments: an overt external argument (implied subject 'he') and an overt internal argument ('his house to be broken into'), satisfying the transitive pattern with both subject and object present.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'allow' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/experiencer) and an internal argument (the infinitival complement), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'allowed' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'his house to be broken into'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Allow' here denotes a punctual act of permission or prevention without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined culmination point, functioning more like a boundary-transition event than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'allowed' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (preventing/blocking an action at a specific moment), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('would not have allowed at that moment'), and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'allowed' denotes a single, punctual permission/prevention event with a defined result state (preventing the breaking in), not a dynamic durative activity without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'would not have allowed his house to be broken into' entails a resulting change of state (the house being broken into or not), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; it is a durative activity or state verb.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'would not have allowed' denotes a dynamic volitional action (preventing/permitting) with an inherent endpoint and internal progression, not a stative condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional, expressing what would have happened under the unfulfilled condition 'if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming'.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'would' of the target gram 'would not have allowed' is negated with 'not', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'He would not have allowed his house to be broken into' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: This is a negative Perfect construction ('would not have allowed') that functions as a claim about the absence of an event in a hypothetical scenario, matching the CRI criterion for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The conditional counterfactual 'would not have allowed' describes a hypothetical past action that did not occur, not a change of state that currently holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical prevention of a single event (house breaking), not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause presents a counterfactual bounded transition ('allow' construed as a completed preventive action) with a clear endpoint, functioning as a discrete hypothetical event in the conditional narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have allowed', forming a counterfactual conditional construction that presents the situation as hypothetical and unrealized rather than asserted as fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' includes a modal auxiliary ('would') preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if condition for modal perfect constructions that are tense-shifted from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' is active voice (agent subject 'He' + transitive verb 'allowed' + object 'his house'), not passive voice; passive morphology requires 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: » Do you think I have come to give peace in the earth ? I tell you , No , instead division .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt direct object; the infinitive phrase 'to give peace' is a purpose adjunct, not an internal argument of 'come'.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I') that functions as an agent with intentional purpose ('to give peace'), not as a theme/patient undergoing change, so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' is unaccusative (the subject 'I' is an internal argument undergoing motion/change of state), not an unergative verb with an external argument agent, and therefore does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have come to give peace' describes directed motion/action toward a defined abstract goal (giving peace), which aligns with the accomplishment criterion of directed action toward a goal that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual transition into a new location/state (arrival at a destination), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'have come at dawn'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a destination) and a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have come' entails a resulting change of state (arrival at a location/condition) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a bounded dynamic event (arrival/movement to a location) with an inherent endpoint and internal progression, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have come' occurs in a rhetorical question ('Do you think I have come...?') expressing actual purpose, not in a counterfactual protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure; the event is presented as factually occurring, not contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have come' is not directly negated; the negation ('No') applies to the entire proposition in the response clause, not to the auxiliary or the target verb itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have come to give peace in the earth' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'have come to give peace' introduces a resulting state or purpose (the subject's arrival and mission to give peace), which is relevant for the subsequent discourse about division, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes arrival, but the clause does not indicate that the mover remains at a destination at the reference time; rather, it expresses purpose ('to give peace'), making this a non-resultative use without a persisting change of location.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have come to give peace' describes a punctual arrival event with a purpose, not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or 'since' markers indicating universal temporal scope.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event (movement to arrival), and 'have come' construes this transition as a completed whole bounded in the past, presenting a single accomplished occurrence rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' appears in a rhetorical question asserting a realized fact about the speaker's purpose, not presenting a hypothetical or non-actual situation; interrogative form alone does not trigger irrealis when the question seeks confirmation of an actual state of affairs.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have come' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle) with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have come' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is a past participle but there is no 'been', and the subject 'I' is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient undergoing it.

FullText: » So he told his gardener , I have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years . I have not found any . So cut it down ! Why should it continue using the soil ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' is intransitive and does not license an internal argument; 'here' is an adverbial locative modifier, not a direct object, so no overt grammatical object is present.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as agent performing directed motion), so it does not meet the unaccusative criteria despite denoting change-of-location.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'coming' denotes manner of motion but licenses an internal argument (the prepositional phrase 'here' functions as a locative complement/goal), and the full construction 'have been coming here' includes an adjunct 'looking for figs on this tree' that indicates a purposive internal relation, failing the criterion of no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have been coming here' describes a repeated, habitual activity over three years without a defined terminal endpoint or culminating result state, making it an atelic, iterative activity rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect continuous 'have been coming here' denotes a durative process phase spanning three years with no punctual boundary transition, failing all set_to_1_if conditions.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have been coming here' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of repeated action over time (three years) that is naturally modified by the 'for three years' adverbial, meeting the criteria for an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect continuous 'have been coming here' construes the event as a durative activity spanning three years, not a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect progressive 'have been coming here' denotes a repeated dynamic activity with internal progression over time, not a static state; it involves volitional engagement and eventive unfolding, failing all set_to_1_if conditions.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target gram 'have been coming here' is a factual statement in the past perfect continuous tense describing an actual, repeated action that occurred over three years, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been coming here' is not negated; negation appears in the subsequent clause 'I have not found any' which modifies a different verb.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the phrasal past time adverb 'for three years', which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect progressive 'have been coming here' with the duration phrase 'for three years' describes an ongoing situation rather than a discrete life-history entry, focusing on the persistence of the activity over time rather than its occurrence as a bounded experience.
DtRes_explanation: The clause describes a repeated action over a duration ('for three years') without indicating that a change of state persists at the reference time; it reports a habitual/iterative past situation rather than a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for three years' and denotes a repeated activity (coming to look for figs) that has extended continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, satisfying the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have been coming here' presents an ongoing, repeated action over three years rather than a single bounded occurrence or completed transition; it is progressive/habitual in aspect, not perfective.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' is a present perfect continuous form asserting a realized, habitual action in the actual world over the past three years, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('coming'), which matches the set_to_1_if condition for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have been coming' uses 'have' + 'been' + present participle (not past participle), and 'coming' is an intransitive verb; this is active progressive aspect, not passive voice morphology, so VcPass is not instantiated.

FullText: » So he told his gardener , I have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years . I have not found any . So cut it down ! Why should it continue using the soil ?
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have not found' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('any'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with two participants.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'find' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as agent/experiencer) and an internal argument (the object 'any' figs), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'found' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('any' as the direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Finding figs involves a directed search process culminating in a defined result state (having found figs), making it an accomplishment; the negation does not change the aspectual class of the verb.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'found' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (discovery/location of figs), and the negated perfect 'have not found' construes this as a boundary event that either occurs or does not occur, meeting the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'found' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint (discovery of figs), and the perfect aspect 'have not found' emphasizes the result state of non-discovery rather than an ongoing dynamic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have not found' is a perfect aspect construction expressing a state resulting from a past event (failure to locate figs over three years), which entails a resulting change of state and is not a single punctual occurrence, thus not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have not found' denotes a bounded perceptual/discovery event with a defined endpoint (the moment of non-discovery), involving dynamic change from a state of searching to a state of having searched without success, which instantiates eventive rather than stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have not found' describes a factual past situation (the speaker has actually looked for figs and did not find them), not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish; it is a statement of what actually occurred, not what would have occurred under different circumstances.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'have' is negated with 'not' in 'have not found', which directly instantiates the ClNegPol feature according to the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have not found any' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have not found' is a negative Perfect that functions as a claim about the absence of the event (finding figs) in the subject's life history over three years, matching the set_to_1_if condition for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'find' describes a search action, and 'have not found' reports a negative outcome over a three-year period without indicating a persisting resultant state; the clause merely reports the absence of a past discovery without a change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have not found any' describes a negative result (absence of figs) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time; it is a punctual/bounded outcome, not a non-terminating situation holding throughout the three-year span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'find' is a transition event, and 'have not found' construes the transition as a completed whole—the speaker asserts the bounded outcome that figs were not discovered over the three-year period.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have not found' asserts a realized negative fact in the actual world (the speaker has searched for three years and found no figs), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have not found' is a simple present perfect (present tense 'have' + past participle 'found') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have not found' is active voice (subject 'I' is the agent performing the action of finding), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'have + been + past participle' structure for VcPass.

FullText: » Here is a descendant of Abraham whom Satan has kept in bonds for eighteen years . Should she be released on the Sabbath ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has kept' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Satan') and an overt direct object ('a descendant of Abraham'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with two participants.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'kept' licenses an external argument (Satan as agent) that actively holds/restrains the internal argument, making it transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has kept' licenses an internal argument ('a descendant of Abraham') and a prepositional phrase ('in bonds'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has kept in bonds for eighteen years' describes a durative process of maintaining restraint over a measurable span of time directed toward a sustained result state (bondage), fitting the accomplishment profile with an 'in/for X time' temporal frame.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has kept' denotes a durative state of continuous holding or restraint over eighteen years, not a punctual transition into a new state; it describes an ongoing process phase rather than a boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has kept' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of sustained engagement (holding in bonds) that unfolds over time ('for eighteen years'), naturally accepts durative modification, and lacks an inherent endpoint or result state, satisfying the activity criteria.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has kept in bonds' entails a resulting change of state (the woman being bound/imprisoned) and describes a durative condition spanning eighteen years, not a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has kept' denotes a dynamic action of restraint or confinement that requires ongoing volitional engagement and active maintenance, not a static state; it describes an eventive situation with internal progression over the eighteen-year period, failing all four set_to_1_if conditions.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'has kept' describes a factual situation (Satan has kept someone in bonds for eighteen years) rather than a counterfactual condition, unfulfilled wish, or contrary-to-fact scenario; it is a statement of actual fact within the narrative.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has kept' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the clause presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Satan has kept in bonds' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverb; 'for eighteen years' is a duration phrase, not a past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'has kept in bonds' introduces a resulting state (the woman currently being in bondage) that is relevant for the subsequent discourse question about her release, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has kept in bonds' describes a change of state (from free to bound) where the undergoer remains in that changed state at the reference time, as evidenced by the temporal phrase 'for eighteen years' indicating the resulting state persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for eighteen years' indicating a situation (being kept in bonds) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Satan has kept in bonds for eighteen years' presents an ongoing state of confinement rather than a bounded transition; the durative adverbial 'for eighteen years' profiles the situation as extended and continuous, not as a single completed event with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has kept' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Satan's ongoing bondage of the woman), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has kept' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'kept') with no tense shift, modal, future marking, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has kept' is active voice (subject Satan is the agent performing the action), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: » O Jerusalem , Jerusalem , you who kill the prophets ! You stone those sent to you . How often I would have gathered your children together , even as a hen gathers her own brood under her wings . But you would not have it !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'would have gathered' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('your children'), instantiating a two-participant transitive event directed from agent to patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gathered' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') as the agent performing the action of gathering, and takes an internal argument ('your children'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'gathered' licenses an internal argument ('your children'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event of gathering children together is a durative process directed toward a defined goal (the assembled group under wings), analogous to 'build a house', and can be measured with an 'in X time' adverbial, satisfying the accomplishment criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'gathered (together)' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of being collected/assembled, can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('would have gathered at that moment'), and represents a definable threshold event rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'gathered (together)' denotes a punctual, telic action with an inherent endpoint (completing the act of gathering), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'would have gathered' entails a resulting change of state (children brought together under wings) and is construed as a durative action rather than a single punctual occurrence, failing both conditions for semelfactive marking.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'would have gathered' denotes a dynamic, volitional action involving internal progression (the act of gathering children together), not a static state or condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have gathered' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional expressing an unfulfilled wish ('How often I would have gathered...'), where the event is understood not to have occurred, reinforced by the contrastive marker 'But you would not have it' indicating the condition was not satisfied.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have gathered' is not negated; negation appears in the subsequent clause 'would not have it' which negates a different verb, not the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'How often I would have gathered your children together' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverb that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'How often' is a frequency interrogative, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the explicit frequency adverb 'How often' indicating repetition or distinct instances, and the verb denotes a bounded activity (gathering) where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience rather than a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have gathered' expresses an unrealized, hypothetical action that did not occur, so no change of state actually resulted or persists at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'How often I would have gathered your children together' expresses a counterfactual or unrealized habitual intention rather than a continuous state or activity that held from a past point to the reference time; it lacks duration adverbials and denotes a repeated but unfulfilled desire, not an actual continuous or habitual series characterizing the time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have gathered' expresses an unrealized, habitual intention ('how often') rather than a single bounded occurrence, and lacks the completion-measure adverbial or narrative sequencing that would mark it as perfective.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have gathered' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with 'have' in a counterfactual construction expressing an unrealized desire or intention, presenting the situation as hypothetical rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have gathered' includes a modal auxiliary ('would') preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if condition for modal perfect constructions that are tense-shifted from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have gathered' is active voice (agent 'I' as subject, patient 'your children' as object), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle morphology of passive construction.

FullText: » The servant soon told his master , ‘ Your order has been carried out but there is still more room . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been carried out'), and the lexical verb 'carry out' licenses both an external argument (agent performing the action, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient 'Your order'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target is a passive construction ('has been carried out') where an external argument (agent) is implied but suppressed, not an unaccusative verb that fails to license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target is a passive construction with an implicit internal argument (the order being carried out), which licenses an internal argument even though the agent is not overt, failing the requirement for no internal argument licensing.
AktAcc_explanation: Carrying out an order involves a durative process directed toward a defined goal or completion state, fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'carry out' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state (the order has been executed), which can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has been carried out' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (the order is now executed), not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint, and thus does not instantiate AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been carried out' entails a resulting change of state (the order is now completed), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; semelfactives must be punctual dynamic occurrences without consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has been carried out' denotes a completed dynamic action (the execution of an order), which is a bounded eventive process involving change and progression, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has been carried out' describes a factual past event that actually occurred (the order was completed), not a counterfactual situation, and does not appear in a counterfactual conditional structure, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional protasis.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been carried out' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the clause presents the event as realized, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'has been carried out' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; 'soon' is a manner/speed adverb, not a past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The passive perfect 'has been carried out' introduces a result state (the order is now completed) that is relevant to the subsequent discourse ('but there is still more room'), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula construction 'has been carried out' describes a completed state rather than encoding a transition with a persisting result state; it reports that the order was executed in the past without indicating a current binding effect or ongoing consequence.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has been carried out' denotes a completed action with a resultant state, not a continuous situation extending to the reference time; there is no duration adverbial or indication of an ongoing habitual series.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Your order has been carried out' presents a bounded, completed transition event (the execution of an order) as a single whole, matching the passive bounded transition pattern exemplified in the positive examples.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been carried out' is a present perfect passive asserting a realized action in the actual world (the order was completed), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been carried out' includes 'been' followed by a progressive/passive participle ('carried out'), matching the set_to_1_if condition for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been carried out' instantiates VcPass: it contains the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle of the transitive verb 'carry', with the grammatical subject 'Your order' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity affected by the action of being carried out).

FullText: » I will go to my father and say : ‘ Father I have sinned against God and against you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'sinned' has an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt internal argument ('against God and against you'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with both subject and object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent) and denotes an agentive action rather than a change-of-state or uncontrolled event, failing to meet any set_to_1_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an internal argument ('against God and against you'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have sinned' denotes a punctual or stative transgression without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined terminal result state, making it more achievement- or state-like than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a punctual transition into a culpable state and can be naturally modified by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'I have sinned today'), meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent result state (the commission of sin), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' entails a resulting change of state (the subject has committed sin and now bears that moral condition), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI definition requiring 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (a transgression) rather than a state that holds over time without change; it involves volitional wrongdoing with an inherent endpoint, not a stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' occurs in a reported speech clause ('say: Father I have sinned') expressing a factual acknowledgment of past wrongdoing, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish, and lacks the conditional or contrastive markers required for ClCntfcl annotation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have sinned' contains no negation marker ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; it is a positive assertion of a past action.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned against God and against you' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have sinned' functions as a claim about the occurrence of sin in the subject's life history, matching the CRI condition for negative Perfects that present events as part of one's experiential past.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' reports a past action within reported speech without indicating that a resulting state (such as guilt, condemnation, or spiritual status) persists at the reference time; it is a communication/confession of past wrongdoing rather than a resultative state change.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned against God and against you' describes a punctual or bounded transgression without duration adverbials, habitual iteration, or 'since' markers indicating continuous extension to the reference time, making it a discrete past event rather than a universal state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have sinned' denotes a stative/unbounded situation (a completed action with present relevance) rather than a transition event construed as a bounded whole; it lacks the bounded-occurrence or transition-targeting semantics required for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' is embedded within direct speech reporting a realized fact (the speaker confessing an actual sin), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; it asserts a completed action in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have sinned' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle) with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have sinned' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'sin'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle of a transitive verb, and the subject 'I' is the semantic agent, not the patient.

FullText: » Father , the son said : ‘ I have sinned against God and against you . I am not fit to be called your son . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'sinned' has an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt prepositional phrase indicating the affected party ('against God and against you'), which satisfies the two-participant transitive event structure with external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as agent performing the action of sinning), which disqualifies it from ASUncc status as the feature requires a verb that does not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an internal argument ('against God and against you'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have sinned' denotes a punctual transgressive act (or series of acts) without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined culmination point, functioning more like an achievement or state than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a punctual transition into a culpable state (crossing a moral/ethical threshold), and the event can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'I have sinned at that moment'), meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent result state (having committed sin), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' entails a resulting change of state (the son is now in a state of having committed sin against God and the father), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI definition requiring 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (a transgression) rather than a state that holds over time without change; it involves volitional wrongdoing with an inherent endpoint, not a stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned' is a factual statement of a past action that actually occurred, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish; it appears in direct speech where the son confesses a real transgression.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have sinned' contains no negation marker ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; the negation in the sentence ('I am not fit') applies to a different clause.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned against God and against you' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial to anchor the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'have sinned' is a positive Perfect expressing the occurrence of an event in the subject's life history without asserting a resulting state, fitting the experiential (DtExp) feature as it answers whether the event has occurred at least once.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'sinned' describes a past action/transgression without indicating that a resulting state persists at the reference time; the clause reports a past moral transgression rather than a current state change, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned' describes a punctual or completed action without duration adverbials, habitual iteration, or a 'since' clause indicating continuous state from a past point to reference time, making it a bounded result rather than a universal duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'sinned' is a transition event (wrongdoing as a completed action) construed as a bounded whole, and 'have sinned' presents this as a single completed occurrence rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' is a present perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the son confesses a sin he has committed), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle) with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have sinned' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'sin'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb, and the subject 'I' is the semantic agent, not the patient.

FullText: » ‘ My son was dead . Now he is alive . He was lost . Now he has been found . The feasting began . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been found'), and the lexical verb 'find' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, the implicit subject 'he'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'has been found' is a passive construction where the sole overt argument ('he') is the logical object/theme, but the verb licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the finding), which disqualifies it from ASUncc status per the negative examples.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'has been found' is a passive construction where the grammatical subject 'he' is the internal argument (patient/theme) of the verb 'find', not the external argument (agent), thus failing the ASUnrg criteria which requires the sole argument to function as an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Being found is a punctual boundary transition (the moment of discovery) rather than a durative process leading to a defined endpoint, analogous to 'arrive' or 'notice'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'found' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (discovery/recovery of something lost), exemplified in the positive cases as an instantaneous discovery regardless of preparatory searching, and the passive construction 'has been found' preserves this achievement semantics.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has been found' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (transition from lost to found), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has been found' entails a resulting change of state (from lost to found), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI definition requiring 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has been found' denotes a bounded perceptual/discovery event with a clear endpoint (the moment of finding), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' describes a factual event that has occurred in the narrative world (contrasted with the prior state 'he was lost'), not a counterfactual or contrary-to-fact situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been found' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' introduces a resulting state (being found/located) that is relevant to the narrative progression, contrasting with the prior state 'he was lost,' rather than presenting the event as part of the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'has been found' is a stative copula (passive with 'be') describing a resulting state rather than encoding a transition; per edge_cases, such constructions do not encode the change itself despite the underlying verb 'find' being a change-of-state verb.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' describes a punctual result or transition into a new state (from lost to found) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has been found' is a passive construction with the transition verb 'find', construed as a completed bounded event (the son was lost, now he has been found), functioning as a discrete step in the narrative sequence that profiles the situation as a single completed occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been found' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context ('Now he has been found'), not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been found' includes 'been' followed by a past participle ('found'), which instantiates the progressive perfect construction pattern specified in the set_to_1_if conditions, making it a tense-shifted form distinct from simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been found' instantiates the passive voice with the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle of the transitive verb 'find', where the grammatical subject 'he' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity that was lost and is now found).

FullText: » Your brother returned home , the servant answered . Your father killed the prize calf because he has returned safely .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has returned' lacks an overt direct object; 'home' is an adverbial complement indicating direction/location, not a grammatical object, and the construction matches the intransitive pattern exemplified by 'He has died.'
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (he) undergoes displacement and no external argument is licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for change-of-location verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has returned' licenses an internal argument (the destination 'home' is implied as the complement of the motion verb), making it transitive rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has returned' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a destination), not a durative process leading toward a goal, analogous to 'have arrived' in the negative examples.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location after absence), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has returned at noon'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a destination) and a defined result state (being back), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has returned' entails a resulting change of state (the brother is now home, having been away), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; semelfactives must be dynamic punctual events without consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes a bounded dynamic event with inherent progression and endpoint (the completion of returning), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has returned' occurs in a causal clause ('because he has returned safely') describing a factual event that actually occurred, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has returned' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the event is presented as occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has returned safely' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; 'safely' is a manner adverb, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'has returned' introduces a resulting state (the brother's safe arrival) that is relevant for the father's subsequent action (killing the calf), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has returned' describes a motion verb (return to a destination) where the mover has moved home and the resulting state of being home persists at the reference time, satisfying the third set_to_1_if condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has returned' denotes a punctual, completed action (arrival at a destination) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and no duration adverbials are present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has returned' is a transition event (arrival/change of state) construed as a completed whole, meeting the criterion for bounded perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has returned' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (the brother has actually returned home), not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has returned' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'has' + past participle 'returned') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has returned' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'return'), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' element and the subject 'he' is the semantic agent, not the patient.

FullText: » He spoke to his father in anger . All these years I have worked like a slave for you . I have never disobeyed you . What have you given me ? Not even a goat for me to have a feast with my friends !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have worked' is intransitive with no overt direct object; the prepositional phrase 'like a slave for you' is an adjunct, not an internal argument, so the two-participant transitive pattern is not instantiated.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'worked' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as an agent performing labor), making it transitive/agentive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have worked' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') functioning as an external argument (agent engaged in labor) with no internal argument (direct or indirect object) licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have worked like a slave for you' describes a durative process of labor directed toward serving the father over many years, constituting an accomplishment with a defined temporal span and implicit result state (service rendered), satisfying the 'in X time' test ('I worked like a slave for you for all these years').
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have worked' denotes a durative activity spanning extended time ('All these years'), not a punctual transition into a new state; it describes an ongoing process phase rather than a boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have worked' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of sustained labor over an extended period ('All these years'), which is homogeneous (any subinterval of working is still working) and can be naturally modified by durative adverbials without implying completion of a bounded goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have worked' is a present perfect expressing a durative activity spanning 'all these years' with a resulting state of servitude, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have worked' denotes a dynamic activity (labor/work) with internal progression and effort over time, not a static state; it describes eventive unfolding rather than a timeless condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have worked' occurs in a factual past statement ('All these years I have worked like a slave for you') describing an event that actually occurred, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have worked' is not negated; the negation 'never' modifies the subsequent clause 'I have never disobeyed you', not the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the phrasal past time adverb 'All these years', which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'I have worked like a slave for you' presents the event as part of the subject's life history across 'all these years,' focusing on the occurrence of the experience rather than a resulting state, which instantiates DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have worked' describes a durative activity (labor over time) without encoding a change of state or a persisting result state; it reports past action without a current binding consequence.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'All these years' indicating a situation (working) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have worked like a slave for you' presents an unbounded, habitual, or durative situation spanning 'all these years' rather than a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have worked' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the speaker's past labor up to the present moment), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have worked' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle) with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have worked' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'work'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient being affected.

FullText: » He spoke to his father in anger . All these years I have worked like a slave for you . I have never disobeyed you . What have you given me ? Not even a goat for me to have a feast with my friends !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'disobeyed' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'disobeyed' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as an agent performing the action of not obeying), making it transitive/agentive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'disobeyed' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'you'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have never disobeyed' denotes a punctual act of compliance/non-compliance rather than a durative process directed toward a defined goal or result state, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'disobeyed' denotes a durative state of non-compliance over an extended period ('never' across 'all these years'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and does not permit modification by a specific time-point adverbial.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have never disobeyed' denotes a negated state of non-action over a bounded lifetime period with an implicit result state (never having performed the action), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds over time.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have never disobeyed' is a negated durative state describing a habitual pattern across time ('all these years'), not a single punctual dynamic occurrence, and thus does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have never disobeyed' denotes a bounded negative event (the absence of disobedience across a time span) rather than a stative situation that holds without dynamic change; it describes a completed state resulting from past events, not an inherent atelic state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have never disobeyed' is a factual statement about past behavior in a complaint, not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have never disobeyed' contains the adverb 'never' which modifies the target gram, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition and instantiating ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have never disobeyed you' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'never' is a negation/frequency adverb, not a past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have never disobeyed' explicitly contains the frequency adverb 'never' and functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, satisfying two set_to_1_if conditions.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'I have never disobeyed you' reports a habitual/iterative absence of disobedience over a period of time rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that persists at reference time; the frequency adverbial 'never' indicates a pattern of non-action without a change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have never disobeyed you' describes a negated discrete event (disobedience) that has not occurred, rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time; it lacks duration adverbials and does not denote a non-terminating situation holding throughout a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have never disobeyed you' presents a stative/unbounded situation (a state of non-disobedience across a period) rather than a bounded transition event, and lacks a completion-measure adverbial or narrative sequencing that would profile it as a single bounded occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have never disobeyed' is a present perfect assertion of a realized fact in the actual world (the speaker's lifelong obedience), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; it contains no modal auxiliary, conditional structure, or irrealis marking.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have never disobeyed' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle) with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have never disobeyed' is active voice (subject 'I' is the agent performing the action), lacking the passive morphology of 'have' + 'been' + past participle required for VcPass.

FullText: » He spoke to his father in anger . All these years I have worked like a slave for you . I have never disobeyed you . What have you given me ? Not even a goat for me to have a feast with my friends !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have given' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with two participants.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an external argument (the implicit 'you' as agent/giver) and an internal argument (the theme 'me'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have given' is transitive and licenses an internal argument (direct object 'me'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have given' here denotes a punctual transfer/transaction (giving a goat) without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of possession (the crossing of a threshold from not-having to having), and the event can be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial (e.g., 'have given me at Christmas'), satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a bounded, punctual transfer event with an inherent endpoint and result state (possession of a gift), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, and lacks the homogeneity required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have given' in the context 'What have you given me?' entails a resulting change of state (possession transfer) and does not construe a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a bounded dynamic event (a completed action of transfer) rather than a stative situation; it involves eventive unfolding and does not hold uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'have given' occurs in a rhetorical question expressing complaint about actual past events, not in a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have given' is modified by the negative adverb 'never' in the preceding clause ('I have never disobeyed you'), and the question 'What have you given me?' is immediately followed by the negation 'Not even a goat', establishing a clear negative polarity context where the giving is construed as not occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'What have you given me?' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have you given' functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if condition for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'What have you given me?' reports a past interaction without entailing that any gift or possession is currently in effect; it is a rhetorical complaint about the absence of gifts rather than a statement that a resulting state persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What have you given me?' expresses a punctual or bounded action (a gift or gifts given at discrete moments) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'What have you given me?' presents a stative result of past giving rather than a bounded transition event; it queries the cumulative outcome of giving over time, not a single completed occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have given' appears in an interrogative clause ('What have you given me?') that seeks confirmation of an actual fact about past actions in the real world, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have given' is a simple present perfect (present-tense 'have' + past participle 'given') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have given' is active voice (subject 'you' is the agent performing the action of giving), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have been + past participle' with the subject as the semantic patient.

FullText: » ‘ We had to celebrate and be happy . Your brother was dead . Now he is alive ! He was lost and now he has been found ! ’ «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been found') and the lexical verb 'find' licenses both an external argument (agent—someone who found him) and an internal argument (patient—'he'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'has been found' is a passive construction where 'find' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent who found him), making it not unaccusative despite the sole overt argument functioning as a theme.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has been found' is a passive construction where the subject 'he' functions as an internal argument (patient/theme) rather than an external argument (agent/initiator), failing the requirement for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has been found' denotes a punctual transition into a result state (being found) without an inherent durative process phase leading to completion, analogous to 'arrived' rather than 'built a house'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been found' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (discovery/recovery of something lost), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has been found at noon'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has been found' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of discovery) and a defined result state (being found), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been found' entails a resulting change of state (from lost to found) and is not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has been found' denotes a bounded, completed perceptual/discovery event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of finding), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has been found' occurs in a factual narrative context describing an actual event that occurred (the brother was lost and now is found), not in a counterfactual conditional, inverted conditional, or unfulfilled wish construction.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been found' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as realized and occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' contains the clausal past time anchor 'now' which functions as a temporal adverb explicitly anchoring the event to the present moment in contrast with the prior state, satisfying the requirement for an overt temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'has been found' introduces a resulting state (the brother is now found/located) that is relevant to the current discourse and celebration, not merely an entry in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' uses a stative copular construction (passive with 'been') that describes a resulting state rather than encoding a transition; per edge_cases, such constructions do not instantiate DtRes even though 'found' denotes a change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' describes a punctual result or transition into a new state (from lost to found) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' presents a bounded transition event (the predicate 'find' denotes a transition from lost to found state) construed as a completed whole, matching the passive bounded transition criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been found' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context, not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been found' includes 'been' followed by a past participle (progressive-like structure in passive voice), which matches the set_to_1_if condition for progressive perfect forms, making it tense-shifted from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been found' instantiates the passive voice with the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle of the transitive verb 'find', where the grammatical subject 'he' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity that was lost and is now found).

FullText: » If you have not been faithful in handling worldly wealth , how can you be trusted with true wealth ?
ASTrns_explanation: The target is a passive construction with 'faithful' (adjective/predicate), not a lexical verb licensing both external and internal arguments; 'faithful' does not encode a two-participant transitive event with an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: This is a copular clause with 'be' where the sole overt argument 'you' functions as the syntactic subject linked to the predicative complement 'faithful', and no external argument is licensed, matching the copular unaccusative criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'have not been faithful' is a stative predicate with an internal argument ('in handling worldly wealth'), which does not instantiate ASUnrg as it licenses a prepositional phrase functioning as an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Being faithful is a stative condition rather than a durative process directed toward a defined goal or culminating endpoint, so it does not qualify as an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'been faithful' denotes a durative state or ongoing condition of behavior rather than a punctual transition into a new state, lacking the instantaneous boundary crossing characteristic of achievements.

FullText: The same applies to you . When you have done all you have been told to do , you say , we are unworthy servants . We have done what we ought to have done . «

FullText: The same applies to you . When you have done all you have been told to do , you say , we are unworthy servants . We have done what we ought to have done . «

AktAcc_explanation: The target gram 'have been told to do' denotes a punctual communicative act (being told/instructed) that does not involve a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it is a single transition into a state of having received instruction.

FullText: The same applies to you . When you have done all you have been told to do , you say , we are unworthy servants . We have done what we ought to have done . «

FullText: The man replied : » I have observed all these things from my youth . «

FullText: Peter said : » Indeed we have left our own to follow you . «

FullText: Zacchaeus stood before Jesus and said : » Lord , I now give half of all my possessions to the poor . If I have defrauded any one I will give back four times the amount taken . «
